coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted green synthesis of multi-functional carbon quantum dots as efficient fluorescence sensor for ultra-trace level monitoring of ammonia in environmental water
coredata.dc:creator: Ganesan, Sivarasan
Kalimuthu, Rajendran
Kanagaraj, Thamaraiselvi
Kulandaivelu, Ravichandran
Nagappan, Rajendiran
Pragasan, Lingassamy Arul
Ponnusamy, Vinoth Kumar
coredata.dc:description: This study reports a facile green preparation of self-assembled multi-functional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) via direct pyrolysis technique coupled with microwave-assisted synthesis using Ziziphus Mauritiana stone biomass (as a bio-resource precursor). The synthesized multi-functional CQDs was characterized using FT-IR, XRD, XPS, TEM, and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques. The results exhibit that the prepared CQDs are spherical-shaped with an average diameter of 2–4 nm and showed bright bluish-green emissions property with stable dispersion and high photostability in the aqueous medium. Furthermore, the emission properties of CQDs were examined by quenched with ammonia (NH3) and other molecules in aqueous media. Results indicated that the developed CQDs showed effective fluorescent for the selective and sensitive detection (sensor) of NH3 with a detection limit of 10 nM. Thus, the presented procedure is a simple, low-cost, efficient, chemical-free synthesis of CQDs and can be applied as selective and sensitive (sensor) monitoring of NH3 concentration in aquatic environmental samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave-assisted synthesis
Ziziphus mauritiana biomass
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence
Ammonia detection
Environmental water

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties of synthesized carbon quantum dots
Effects of pH and NaCl on the stability of ZMS-CQDs
Fluorometric detection of NH3 with various interferences using ZMS-CQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research work was supported financially by the grant MOST110-2113-M-037-009 - from Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan , and also funded by the Research Center for Environmental Medicine, Kaohsiung Medical University , Kaohsiung, Taiwan from “The Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project” by the Ministry of Education (MOE) in Taiwan.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphical scheme of ZMS-CQDs synthesis process and NH3 detection processes.
(a) TEM micrograph of ZMS-CQDs in the 20 nm range (Inside: SAED pattern of the TEM image); (b) TEM micrograph of ZMS-CQDs in the 2 nm range; (c) Particle size plot of ZMS-CQDs.
(a) FTIR spectra; (b) XRD pattern of ZMS-CQDs; (c) Complete XPS spectrum of ZMS-CQDs; (d) C1s peaks signal from XPS analysis.
(a) Time-resolved fluorescence lifetime studies of ZMS derived CQDs; (b) Zeta potential of ZMS-CQDs.

UV–visible absorption spectrum of emission (λem = 475 nm) and excitation (λex = 375 nm) and spectra of ZMS derived CQDs (Inset: (i) The digital image of ZMS derived CQDs solution under visible and (ii) UV irradiations).
3D Contour plot of the fluorescence excitation vs. emission spectra of ZMS-CQDs.

(a) Photoluminescence spectra of ZMS-CQDs at wide ranges of pH (2–12); (b) Photoluminescence intensity at different concentrations of NaCl; (c) Fluorescence spectra of ZMS-CQDs in the occurrence of various volatiles interferences with responses of NH3 ions; (d) Fluorescence emission spectrum of ZMS-CQDs in the existence of various concentrations of NH3 (0–250 μL).
Fluorescence intensity of CQDs in the occurrence of different concentration of NH3 ions.
Comparison of different CQDs used for ammonia detection in the previous reports.

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted green synthesis of multi-functional carbon quantum dots as efficient fluorescence sensor for ultra-trace level monitoring of ammonia in environmental water

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors are equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study reports a facile green preparation of self-assembled multi-functional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) via direct pyrolysis technique coupled with microwave-assisted synthesis using Ziziphus Mauritiana stone biomass (as a bio-resource precursor). The synthesized multi-functional CQDs was characterized using FT-IR, XRD, XPS, TEM, and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques. The results exhibit that the prepared CQDs are spherical-shaped with an average diameter of 2–4 nm and showed bright bluish-green emissions property with stable dispersion and high photostability in the aqueous medium. Furthermore, the emission properties of CQDs were examined by quenched with ammonia (NH3) and other molecules in aqueous media. Results indicated that the developed CQDs showed effective fluorescent for the selective and sensitive detection (sensor) of NH3 with a detection limit of 10 nM. Thus, the presented procedure is a simple, low-cost, efficient, chemical-free synthesis of CQDs and can be applied as selective and sensitive (sensor) monitoring of NH3 concentration in aquatic environmental samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia (NH3) is a widely used toxic chemical agent (gas or liquid form) in agro-fertilizer industries. It is often contaminated into the environment as a secondary product of anaerobic fermentation, metabolic process, and fossil fuel combustion processes (Ganiga and Cyriac, 2016; Meng et al., 2017; Supchocksoonthorn et al., 2019a). It causes several toxic problems in the human body, including irritation in the throat, eyes, and skin, affecting breathing routes. It is one of the unhealthiest gases that cause a serious detrimental effect if inhaled in routine life. The United States environmental protection agency (USEPA) has proscribed a safe exposure level of NH3 is 1.17 mMol for 8 h per day exposure and >2.93 mMol for acute toxicity (Supchocksoonthorn et al., 2019b). In a marine environment, the presence of free ammonia (non-ionized form) in water is harmful to aquatic animals at >0.27 mMol. Also, trace level of ammonia exposure in the environment causes high impacts on living species (Eddy, 2005; Zhu et al., 2019). Therefore, detecting trace level ammonia is vital in environmental water (Wang et al., 2015).

In the past, electrochemical-based approaches were commonly employed for the detection of NH3 in the environment. Typically, metal oxide-based semiconductor materials such as SnO2 (Wang et al., 2001), ZnO (Donarelli et al., 2016), CdTe/ZnS quantum dots (QDs) (Yi, 2016), and Ti–In2O3 (Guo and Pan, 2006) were applied for the electrochemical detection of NH3 concentrations. However, in recent times, more attention has been addressed to the advancement of electrochemical sensors using organometallic frameworks and carbon-based polymeric nanomaterials such as rGO/Ir (Zhang et al., 2019), Pd–Cu–Mo2C (Vilian et al., 2021), Ag-CNT (Baciu et al., 2017), Pt-PPy/NiF (Zhang et al., 2020), SnO2/Pd/RGO (Qi et al., 2014), and PEDOT:PSS/MWCNT composites (Sharma et al., 2014). Notably, an electrochemical sensor consisting of ZnO/Polyaniline showed good sensitivity towards ammonia detection compared with a sensor comprising of ZnO alone (Zhu et al., 2016). However, most reported electrodes indicated several disadvantages such as less significance with species selectivity, interferences with organic volatiles species may cause strong influence, and hygienic handlings procedures (Ganiga and Cyriac, 2016).

Alternatively, the fluorescence detection method provides higher sensitivity and selectivity compared to other available detection methods. Recently, researchers grabbed more attention to utilize the naturally existing sources or artificially prepared carbon quantum dots (CQDs) materials as a fluorescence sensor material because of its unique properties such as aqueous solubility, ultra-small size impressive optical and chemical stability, high quantum yield, low toxicity-high resistance from photo-bleaching, and biocompatibility (Li et al., 2021; Nazri et al., 2021; Rajendran et al., 2019; Supchocksoonthorn et al., 2019b). Previously, fluorescent CQDs from sucrose (as starting material) was applied to detect malathion contamination levels in the real samples (Liang et al., 2021). Several studies explained the excellent utilization of CQDs for various environmental pollutants detection in aqueous media. Li et al. (2020) demonstrated the successful utilization of polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) polymer blended CQDs as fluorescent paper to detect folic acid (Li et al., 2020). Wang et al. (2021) synthesized the dual emission fluorescent CQDs from zeolite using the co-precipitation method, and it was applied to detect the lead ions in the environmental water (Wang et al., 2021).

So far, limited studies have focused on the sustainability of CQDs as a fluorescent probe in detecting NH3 sensing systems (Chen et al., 2016; Supchocksoonthorn et al., 2019b; Travlou et al., 2017). Therefore, exploring a competent approach towards the sensitive and selective detection of ammonia by using a chemical-free synthesis of CQDs as a fluorescent probe is essential to overcome the toxic effects of chemically synthesized CQDs using conventional heavy metal ions, which causes severe health and environmental problems during biological applications (Ibrahim et al., 2018).

Alternatively, Supchocksoonthorna et al. (2019a, b) performed natural biomass-derived (Sesame seeds) CQDs to detect ammonia in gas form. The Indian native species Ziziphus mauritiana stone (ZMS) belongs to the genus Rhamnaceae (Freitas et al., 2019). It is a widely available semi-arid tree in the subtropical and Mediterranean regions like Asia, Africa, and Australia. (Boudechiche et al., 2019). These most beneficial economic products are valuable medicinal leaves and antioxidants-rich edible fruits (Rahman et al., 2021). Hence, this study attempts to execute and develop a proficient method for the selective and sensitive detection of NH3 toxicants in the liquid phase using ZMS derived CQDs as a fluorescence agent.

In this study, a new self-assembled multi-functional CQDs were synthesized using ZMS biomass via green synthetic methodology. The lignocellulosic carbon-rich ZMS stone was applied as bioresource material to fabricate a sensitive fluorescent sensor for ammonia detection in environmental water samples. Complete evaluation of the structural, optical, and physiological properties of the green synthesized ZMS-CQDs were examined and discussed (Fig. 1 ) in detail. For the first time, this work reports the performances of photoluminescence and application as an NH3 sensor using the novel biomass-based chemical-free synthesized ZMS-CQDs.

The dry biomass, namely the Indian jujube (Ziziphus mauritiana) fruit shell, was collected from the local market, Coimbatore city, India. Liquid ammonia (25%), dimethyl sulfoxide, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide, Triton X-100, dichloromethane, and ethanol were procured from Sigma-Aldrich. All other supporting salt materials such as NaNO2, NaNO3, NaPO4, C6H14N4O2, C6H9N3O2, and C9H11NO3 were purchased from Xilong Chemicals (Shanghai, China). Pure water was produced using the UNISS pure water system (Taiwan). All metal precursors were prepared via a stoichiometric quantity of salt and deionized distilled water with 1 × 10−5 M concentration for the experiments. MWCO 500–1000Da dialysis membrane was obtained from Spectrum Laboratories. The glass containers used for the reactions were washed by the solution mixture of HNO3 and HCl with the ratio of 1:3% v/v and then cleaned with pure water.

Firstly, a simple carbonization process-based procedure was utilized to prepare efficient biochar from Ziziphus mauritiana seeds (ZMS). Briefly, the dried seeds were collected and washed with ethanol three times and then dried at 80 °C for 48 h in a hot-air oven. The fine powder was obtained from grinding the stones-shaped dried ZMS seeds. Then, 10 g of seed powder was carbonized in a muffle furnace at 750 °C for 12 h under N2 (1 atm pressure) atmosphere. The obtained black product was quantified (5.8 g) and washed several times with pure water to remove excess volatile impurities and then dried at 105 °C for 3 h. Then, 2 g of dried black carbon product was dispersed in 10 mL of pure water in a standard microwave tube. Finally, the mixture solution was treated in a microwave oven for 20 min (150 W). After the microwave treatment, the mixture solution was cooled to obtain a brown precipitate representing CQDs' yield.

For the extraction process, 0.5 g of fine CQDs were dissolved in 50 mL of absolute ethanol solvent, and it was centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 10 min. Then, the solvent was evaporated by drying at 80 °C, and the supernatant liquid was collected. The residue was again dispersed in 10 mL of pure water and transferred into a glass tube with a cap. The glass tube was placed in a microwave reactor, and then it was heated at 250 °C with 200 PSI pressure for 5 min. After that, the solution was cooled, and the supernatant was filtered using a syringe filter with 0.22 μm pores. Finally, the obtained CQDs solution expressed strong blue fluorescent under UV light. The purified solution was further dehydrated in a hot air oven at 85 °C for 6 h to obtain a brown-colored fine powder of CQDs.

Various instrumental techniques were adopted to analyze the synthesized CQDs material. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of as-synthesized CQDs were taken with 250 kV accelerating voltage in FEI-Tecnai 300kv TEM. The infrared spectra were recorded using a Fourier-transform FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker Tensor 27, PerkinElmer). The crystalline structure was determined by powder X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis recorded with Cu-Kα1 radiation (λ. = 1.5418 Å) in Brucker D8-diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using PHI 5000 VersaProbe (ESCA model). The XPS survey spectrum was recorded with 0.5 step size and the mass-energy of 50 eV. UV–visible spectra were recorded (200–1100 nm) using a Shimadzu UV-1601 spectrophotometer (Japan). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the solution containing the synthesized CQDs were recorded under 450 W Xenon lamps by Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluoromax-4 fluoro photometer. The diluted solution of CQDs with water was placed in 1.0 cm pathlength quartz cuvettes. The time-correlated single-photon-counting technique was adopted to measure time-resolved fluorescence decays using IBH, Model 5000U (Glasgow, UK). The second harmonic output (375 nm) was generated by a flexible harmonic generator (Spectra-Physics, GWU 23 PS), and the samples were excited by using a vertically polarized (375 nm) laser.

In this work, Ziziphus mauritiana stones (ZMS, lignocellulosic biomass) was utilized as a carbon source to prepare multi-functional CQD due to the rich-carbon content and high bioresource availability of ZMS. ZMS were subjected to a controlled pyrolysis process and microwave-assisted synthesis. Both pyrolysis and microwave-based synthetic procedures were widely recognized as eco-friendly synthesis methods for preparing CQDs due to their easy accessibility to produce high purity with high yield (Supchocksoonthorn et al., 2019b). The pyrolysis process of brown ZMS seeds at 750 °C for 2 h, achieved 65.6% yield. Further, microwave-assisted synthesis yields about 21.3% of final CQDs. After washing, centrifugation, and refining via membrane dialysis process, the pure CQDs material was obtained and indicated blue emission under UV lamp. After the complete dehydration process, the dried ZMS-CQDs powder was used for physicochemical characterizations and evaluated its application towards fluorescent NH3 sensor in the aqueous medium. The overall CQDs synthesis and its NH3 sensing application are shown in Fig. 1.

The morphology and size distribution of ZMS derived CQDs were evaluated via TEM and dynamic light scattering (DLS) studies, and the results are shown in Fig. 2 . High resolution (HR) TEM micrographs depict that the particles were agglomerated evenly and showed spherical-like shapes with a particle diameter of ∼2.4 nm. Fig. 2a (inset) indicates the SAED (selected area electron diffraction) pattern of the prepared ZMS-CQDs, confirming the amorphous nature of CQDs. In-depth analysis of HR-TEM at 2 nm focus point showed the high crystallinity of CQDs (Fig. 2b) with a lattice parameter of 0.39 nm, which may indicate the similarity to the crystal phase of graphene (Bandi et al., 2016). The DLS size distribution results showed that the green synthesized ZMS-CQDs particles are uniform in shape with diverse particle sizes ranging between 2–4 nm.

The FTIR analysis was further investigated to confirm the oxygen-holding functional groups on the CQDs' surface (Fig. 3 a). The absorption peak presented at 1631 cm−1, ensuring the carbonization process-induced CC bond formation on the CQDs surface. The absorption peaks that appeared at 1670 cm−1, 2930 cm−1, and 3293 cm−1 are attributed to the presence of CO, C–N, and O–H/N–H stretching vibrations, respectively (Supchocksoonthorn et al., 2019b). The bands present at 1527 and 1230 cm−1 are attributed to the symmetric and C–O–C and C–O group asymmetric stretching, respectively (Jiang et al., 2018). The appearance of bluish-green color emission of prepared CQDs may be due to the presence of oxygen-holding functional groups, such as C–O–C, C–N, and CO on CQDs’ surface (Chandra et al., 2013).

The crystalline phase purity of as-prepared ZMS-CQDs was elucidated via XRD analysis. Fig. 3b depicts the XRD crystalline profile of the bluish green-emitting CQDs, a broad peak at 23.8° indicating the (002) plane and amorphous crystalline nature (Radhakrishnan and Panneerselvam, 2018). The interlayer d-spacing value was 0.39 nm, which is slightly more significant than the graphite d-spacing value of 0.34 nm. This broad peak also indicates the amorphous nature that resulted from the interruption of oxygen presenting functional groups. The greater interspacing distance of CQDs (0.42 nm) than graphite (0.34 nm) may be attributed to the incorporation of the defect in the skeleton plane of CQDs (Rajendran et al., 2019).

XPS analysis was performed to confirm the chemical composition and functionalities of as-prepared CQDs. XPS full survey spectrum indicates the presence of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s peaks centered at 285, 399, and 532 eV (Fig. 3c). Significantly, the peak attributed at 285.27 eV demonstrates the containing of C atom in the graphitic shape, denoting that the synthesized CQDs owned sp2 carbons predominantly (Fig. 3d) (Alam et al., 2015). The atomic ratio of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s was 66.3%, 7.1%, and 26.6%, respectively. The C 1s spectrum splits into four different peaks at 284.6, 285.1 eV, and 287.1 correspond to the various chemical functionalities of carbon, such as CC/C–C, C–OH/C–O–C, and C–O/C–N (Fig. 3d) (Shchukarev and Korolkov, 2004). FT-IR and XPS results indicated that as-synthesized CQDs possess oxygen functional groups such as O–H, CO, and COOH. The existence of the N element plays an essential role behind the high fluorescence of ZMS-CQDs by inducing an electron in the conduction band and increasing the shift in the Fermi level (Wang and Hu, 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of synthesized carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para:
The absorption, transmittance properties, and quantum confinement effects of CQDs were recorded using UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy, and results are shown in Fig. 4 a. As shown in Fig. 4a, an UV–Vis absorption spectrum at 360 nm confirms the presence of π–π* and n–π* corresponds to CO band and conjugated CC bands transitions, respectively. Fig. 4b shows that the negative range of zeta potential value (−17.36 mV) might be due to the existence of negative charges from the functional groups like COO− and CO− on the CQDs (Rajendran et al., 2019). Thoroughly dispersed CQDs aqueous solution shows the highest emission intensity at 475 nm while excited at 375 nm. Fig. 5 (inset) exhibits light yellow and bluish green emission properties for the corresponding solution at daylight and UV light (365 nm). In a distribution, full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the emission wavelength is calculated as 100 nm, which is consistent with previous reports, and to some extent, the intensity is relatively narrow with FWHM, which also confirms the CQDs were distributed with narrow and uniform size (Baragau et al., 2020; Liang et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2017). The 3D contour plot indicates (Fig. 6 ) that the excitation and emission crosslinking spectrum showed the highest intensity between 500 and 300 nm for excitation and 650 and 400 nm for emission. These adjustable emission properties of CQDs are mainly because the CQDs possess various particle sizes and organic emissive sites present over CQDs' surfaces. Additionally, the emission behavior of the CQDs was provided by fluorescence lifetime analysis. Fig. 5 shows the decay of CQDs, which sufficiently fitted with the following equation (Sachdev and Gopinath, 2015);
(1)
I
( t )
= I
( 0 )
∑
i = 1
n
α i
e x p
(
− t /
τ i
)
where, I = intensity, time = t; αi = pre-exponential factor and τi = the excited state lifetime fluorescence of the component (i); n = amount of non-emissive species, respectively. The χ2 values (1.03) were used to check the goodness of the fit. The corresponding amplitude of τ1 and τ2 lifetime was calculated as α1 = 4.13 ns (82.71) and α2 = 11.32 ns (11.45). In addition, the photostability of CQDs was studied under UV light (365 nm) for 100 min continuously in the aqueous medium (Fig. 4). The results show that no significant change was observed on the CQDs fluorescence intensity, implying that the synthesized CQDs owned the highest photostability. The high polarity of aqueous makes it an absolute medium for CQDs with strong fluorescence emission (Sachdev and Gopinath, 2015).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH and NaCl on the stability of ZMS-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the pH stability of synthesized CQDs, it was dispersed in an aqueous solution with pH levels ranging from 2 to 12. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the fluorescence intensity was significantly increased upon increasing the pH towards neutral. On the other hand, the peak intensity decreased slowly after increasing the pH towards the alkaline pH conditions. Hence, the optimal pH for ammonia sensing using ZMS-CQDs was set at 7.0. And the stability of ZMS-CQDs was also studied at pH 7.0 with different ionic strength concentrations (0–2.0 M NaCl). Fig. 7b shows no apparent changes in fluorescence intensity at the examined ionic strength. The above results demonstrate that as-synthesized CQDs are highly stable and tolerant in the elevated saline (ionic) solution, facilitating a latent probe for bio-imaging and sensing functions in biological systems at physiological pH (Li et al., 2012).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric detection of NH3 with various interferences using ZMS-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The highly stable CQDs derived from ZMS was applied as a fluorescent probe towards fluorescence sensing application. Different inorganic salts and amino acids such as NH3, NaNO2, NaNO3, NaPO4, arginine, histidine, and tyrosine were examined for fluorescence sensing. Fig. 7c indicates that NH3 can quench the CQDs fluorescence intensity significantly compared with other target interferences. It clearly demonstrates that as-synthesized CQDs can be applied for the NH3 detection as a fluorescent label in the aqueous medium without experiencing any influences with other target interferences. The quantitative analysis of NH3 was executed by observing variations in the photoluminescence intensity of CQDs in different concentrations of ammonium ions. Fig. 7d shows the maximum emission of CQDs at 475 nm constantly decreased when increasing NH3 concentration into CQDs solution until equilibrium at 250 μL of NH3 addition. It can clearly be noticed that only NH3 can show apparent quenching of the photoluminescence of CQDs, while the effects of other analytes were insignificant to be neglected. These results indicate the high selectivity of carbon quantum dots towards detecting NH3 in an aqueous solution. The existence of O–H, COO-, and NH2 groups on the surface of the CQDs is the main reason for selective interaction between CQDs and NH3 ions among the other environmental sample interferences such as inorganic salts and amino acids.

The quantitative analysis of ammonia was estimated by establishing the calibration curve under various spiked concentrations of NH3 in CQDs solution using a fluorescence spectrometer. Fig. 8 shows an excellent linear correlation associated with the concentration of NH3 in the range between 10 and 80 nMol with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9877 and detection limit of 10 nMol for NH3. The strong photoluminescence quenching property of NH3, and rapid fluorescence response of ZMS derived CQDs material propose an excellent application towards real-time water samples analysis. The fluorescence quantum yield was calculated as 14.6% for CQDs (λex = 395 nm) concerning quinine sulfate as the standard. This obtained yield was considered to be high compared with other CQDs derived biomaterials (Hoan et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2014; Kalaiyarasan et al., 2019; Surendran et al., 2020). One of the main reasons for this effective quantum yield may be the presents of nitrogen functional groups (amide bonds), which are marked from FTIR (3147.5 cm−1, 1237.2 cm−1) and XPS (399 eV) analysis. It has been reported that the nitrogen group of CQDs makes a favorable key resource to enhance their photoluminescence (Bandi et al., 2016; Feng et al., 2016). Based on the previous reports, Table 1 compares different CQDs obtained from various synthetic methodologies and their efficiency towards ammonia detection.

In summary, a simple and efficient CQDs were developed via a chemical-free synthesis process using ZMS as a carbon-rich source in the aqueous medium. The obtained ZMS-CQDs material's characterizations results were well supported for selective fluorescence sensing applications. The bluish-green emission properties of CQDs showed high stability in the aqueous medium. Among the various analytes tested, the synthesized ZMS-CQDs showed high selectivity for NH3 via drastic quenching of fluorescence intensity. Hence, it could use as a selective and sensitive probe for NH3 with a detection limit of 10 nMol in the aqueous medium. The obtained material possesses the merits of selectivity and could adsorb the analytes via surface functionalities of ZMS-CQDs and showed enhanced detection sensitivity. The developed synthesis procedure of the ZMS-CQDs was very convenient, greener, and economically feasible and can be applied as an excellent method for detecting NH3 concentration in real aquatic environments.

Sivarasan Ganesan : Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Writing - Original Draft. Rajendran Kalimuthu : Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Visualization. Thamaraiselvi Kanagaraj : Validation, Software, Writing - Original Draft. Ravichandran Kulandaivelu : Resources, Conceptualization, Visualization. Rajendiran Nagappan : Resources, Conceptualization, Visualization. Lingassamy Arul Pragasan : Resources, Visualization. Vinoth Kumar Ponnusamy : Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research work was supported financially by the grant MOST110-2113-M-037-009- from Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan, and also funded by the Research Center for Environmental Medicine, Kaohsiung Medical University, Kaohsiung, Taiwan from “The Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project” by the Ministry of Education (MOE) in Taiwan.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Alam et al., 2015

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots from cabbage with down- and up-conversion photoluminescence properties: excellent imaging agent for biomedical applications
Green Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Alam, A.-M., Park, B.-Y., Ghouri, Z.K., Park, M., Kim, H.-Y., 2015. Synthesis of carbon quantum dots from cabbage with down- and up-conversion photoluminescence properties: excellent imaging agent for biomedical applications.
